statusLine: WTI holds above $105 amid steady US production gains and significant inventory draws, while Middle East shipping disruptions keep risk premiums elevated across Americas crude markets.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets showed resilience in the week ending May 16, with WTI climbing 0.4% week-on-week to $105.42/bbl despite robust domestic production reaching 13.71 million bpd. Commercial crude inventories fell sharply by 8.6 million barrels, signaling strong demand absorption even as US output continues near record levels. The drawdown, combined with a 4.1 million barrel decline in gasoline stocks, reflects seasonal demand patterns as the driving season approaches.

The WTI-Brent spread widened to -$3.84, indicating relatively tight Americas crude supplies compared to international markets. This discount reflects ongoing pipeline constraints limiting US exports, particularly affecting Canadian heavy crude (WCS) which faces additional transportation bottlenecks. US retail gasoline prices rose 4.7 cents to $4.628/gallon, while diesel held steady, suggesting refined product markets are tightening despite adequate distillate inventories.

Critical maritime advisories across Middle Eastern waterways pose ongoing risks to global crude flows, though direct impact on Americas supply routes remains limited. Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz continue disrupting tanker traffic, supporting crude price floors globally. The Panama Canal remains the key Americas chokepoint, with no current operational restrictions reported.

Brazil's pre-salt production and Guyana's rapidly expanding Stabroek output continue supporting regional supply security, while Venezuela's sanctioned barrels remain largely offline. Canadian oil sands face persistent pipeline constraints, though Trans Mountain expansion progress may ease WCS discounts later in 2026.
keyPoints: WTI gained 0.4% week-on-week to $105.42/bbl with US production at 13.71 million bpd
Commercial crude stocks fell 8.6 million barrels, indicating strong demand despite high output
WTI-Brent spread at -$3.84 reflects regional supply constraints and export limitations
US gasoline prices rose 4.7 cents to $4.628/gallon as driving season approaches
Critical maritime advisories in Middle East keep global risk premiums elevated
Strategic Petroleum Reserve holdings steady at 81.1 million barrels
